## How Gokul Rajaram Thinks

*Synthesis rebuilt 2026-05-18 directly from 6 raw transcripts using citation-or-drop discipline — every principle below cites ≥2 verbatim passages from the source corpus. Generated by scripts/synthesize-from-source.py.*

**Operator-investor obsessed with durability, customer behavior, and decisions with one owner**

### First Principles

**1. Remarkable product beats all distribution.** If the core product isn't 10-100x better than the alternative, no go-to-market motion can save the company. Gokul looks for what is genuinely remarkable or jaw-dropping at the product's core before anything else.

> "if there is not a remarkable product all the go to marketing distribution in the world will not save you. So that's I look for what the remarkability is in the core product or value proposition of the company. Is it 10x 100x better than the alternative?" — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "Google was a remarkable product. Google was definitely a philosophy of build it remarkably and they will come." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "the first product I got exposed to actually which I didn't know about was a product called Caribou... this thing had 100x more storage and this really epitomizes Larry and Sergey's philosophy which was basically built the best technology on the planet." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

**2. You cannot be a single-product company.** Durable companies layer multiple products, where product #2 emerges naturally from product #1. Some products exist for profit, others for retention — and you must be explicit about which is which.

> "you have to have you cannot be a single product company uh you've got to make sure and and most importantly Your product number two needs to emanate very naturally. It can't be like this completely separate product." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "Some products are good for making money. Profit they're part of the profit pool and some are good for retention. Companies need to be very clear which are the profit pool products and which are the retentive products. If you confuse the two your teams don't know and they're built for the wrong outco" — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "multi product they can't just be a single product and go they will need to have a second product I'm sure of that they will need to have a second product" — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

**3. Decisions need one owner, not consensus.** Consensus is a failure mode for important decisions because nobody truly owns the outcome. The person accountable for executing the decision should also be the person who makes it — three hands raised means zero ownership.

> "three of you raising your hands means that there is no decision maker" — *How to Improve Decision-Making Using SPADE*

> "three owners means no owner and he literally entered the meeting and sent us out of the room" — *Square Defangs Difficult Decisions*

> "consensus doesn't work. Consensus doesn't work as a decision making tool, especially for very important decisions... because there's no owner, there's no case of ownership." — *Prolific Angel Investor Gokul Rajaram — SPADE*

**4. Outcomes are customer behavior changes.** Every product decision should be grounded in a hypothesis about how a specific customer behavior will change. Behaviors are leading indicators of business outcomes — features without a behavior-change hypothesis shouldn't ship.

> "outcomes I think are what define the best product people and outcomes have to be defined in the form of customer behavior. I strongly believe that the be because customer behaviors are leading indicators for every business outcomes." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "you should not let any feature go out if there's not a clear hypothesis behind this feature. And the hypothesis has to be articulated in the form of a customer behavior change." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "everything you do or build should be attuned to the goal of what customer state change does it lead to? What customer behavior change does it lead to?" — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

**5. Founders need authentic, lived missions.** Starting a company because you want to start a company is the canonical mistake. The best companies emerge from founders solving a problem they personally lived — that authenticity is what keeps them going through the inevitable near-death moments.

> "I started a company because I wanted to start a company. I was at Google. I saw a lot of people leaving to start companies and I said you know it's something I want to do... we started a company for the sake of starting a company." — *Gokul Rajaram on Starting a Company in the Age of AI*

> "unless it's something you're really passionate about you're going to abandon it. Quit it." — *Gokul Rajaram on Starting a Company in the Age of AI*

> "the most important thing I look for is founder authenticity... my first question to every founder is tell me your founding story. Why did you decide to start this company?" — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

**6. Durability comes from structural moats.** In an era when code is essentially free, software companies need multiple structural moats to be durable. Gokul scores companies across eight: data, workflow, regulatory, distribution, ecosystem, network, physical infrastructure, and scale — four or more is secure.

> "I call it the eight modes. The first mode is data mode... Second is the workflow mode... regulatory mode... distribution mode... Ecosystem mode... network mode... physical infrastructure... scale mode... I think anything four or more, you're pretty damn secure." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "you need to have a few things around durability. One, you need to have ownership of a scarce asset... Second, you might need to you might basically own a control point... Third, you want to maybe have hardware which is hard to replace. Fourth, maybe you want to be part of an essential workflow. Fift" — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "the halflife of software today is so short that unless you're one of these things that make it durable" — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

**7. Go full-stack or stay small.** For vertical software, single-function plays cap out. The companies that scale to $10B+ own the entire stack for their vertical — like Service Titan with 32 products. Ambition to replace the whole system, not just one workflow layer, is the bar.

> "I think as soon as you do vertical I don't think you can do a a one function within a vertical. I think what you want to see there is the ambition and ability to truly own the full stack, build the whole product for the vertical." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "Service Titan, for example, is a canonical example... they have 32 different products. And even after selling 32 different products and really being at least in the US for any service pro service like phys field services company, they are the canonical company" — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "your ambition has to be to replace the entire system... So every company I know is now trying to figure out how do I build the entire platform and not just a system that does some workflows." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

**8. Self-serve forces better product.** If a customer can't onboard without talking to a human, the product isn't done. Self-serve forces real work on onboarding and time-to-delight, opens the aperture to millions of customers, and surprisingly attracts the most sophisticated power users.

> "The definition of self-s serve is the customer can onboard not just use but onboard and use the product without ever talking to or engaging with a single member of the employee base at the company." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "turns out the self-s served customers were the most sophisticated users because if you do something that's interesting, there's all these small agencies, entrepreneurs, hustlers... they exploit the system in ways that you never you never even know" — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "Larry would be disappointed... His goal was not to again he didn't care about the revenue. He cared that Google is involved in serving every single ad on the planet" — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

**9. Judgment is the AI-era superpower.** When AI can produce infinite code and content, the scarce skill is editorial judgment — knowing what to build, what to ship, and what to reject. The best PMs and designers are reducers, not adders.

> "The one thing I think that's going to be truly future proof is judgment. Why? Because what is the biggest challenge you have when you have thousand AI engineers writing code? You have the big challenge of AI slop." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "in an era when you can do everything, the question is which of these things matter and you should truly do on the product side is judgment around what needs to be built and evaluating the output." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "Jack called the product manager role product editor. Why? Because he believed rightly so that the role of the product manager is not to add more features. Any of us can look at a product and say here's 10 things you should build. The best fe the best designers, the best product people edit down thin" — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

**10. Push risk to the transaction, not the gate.** Great products move risk evaluation from upfront gatekeeping to real-time per-event review. Square accepted 95% of merchants and ran risk per transaction. AdSense killed its approval system and reviewed pages only after 100 impressions. Friction-free onboarding wins.

> "Square instead said we are going to accept 95% and but what they did was they put risk at the transaction level. So they accepted you as a person as a business but then once you started processing transactions they would then run machine learning models" — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "Sergey... said okill all this. So we had literally spent half of our engineering team building this complex approval system with ops and so on... instead do it in real time for every page that loads" — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "the square thing was exactly move risk from the business level to a transaction level. The same with AdSense. Move risk from the publisher level... be reactive and solve it when it needs to be solved at that point versus waiting." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

### Contrarian Positions

**1. The SaaS apocalypse is an overreaction.** Public markets have decided every software company is going to zero because code is becoming free. Gokul argues this paints all software with the same brush and is 100% an overreaction — durable companies with multiple moats are being mispriced.

> "the public market has decided that since code is becoming free uh at the low end and becoming much easier to generate and create at the high end uh the market decided that every software company is going to zero. I think this is 100% an overreaction because not all software companies are created equ" — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "I think everything has been painted with the same brush at this point. It is absolutely an overreaction." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "I think the software public markets are not distinguished between these two types of companies. Companies where the half level data is low and where you can actually have you can literally take half of the value of this company and put it onto an AI company that sits next to it." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

**2. Brand is no longer a strong moat in B2B.** Against the consensus that brand matters more as technology commoditizes, Gokul argues businesses are much more rational than consumers, and AI-era switching costs going to zero will gut brand as a defensible moat in B2B software.

> "I haven't talked about brand. I think brand is no longer a strong word. I explicitly excluded brand." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "Businesses are much more rational uh in thinking about it. Less less irrational I think uh and the alternatives are going to be much stronger." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "switching cost is just going to go to essentially zero because over the next one or two years ability to port data your data as a business or consumer from any ecosystem to another ecosystem is going to be very easy" — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

**3. Price doesn't matter for great seed-stage companies.** Against the value-investor instinct to anchor on entry price, Gokul argues that at seed/sub-$100M, if you're right about the company, price almost doesn't matter. Price destroys returns mainly at B+ stage.

> "price almost doesn't matter if you're right about the company. So, I think you just invest at whatever the price is. I for example I invested in the seed round of fair at back in the this is about 8 nine years ago 20 million which is very expensive for a seed round at that time" — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "I think B+ that's when price starts destroying returns... at subund million and maybe that's an arbitrary number you can if the company is good you'll make money regardless." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

**4. Hire your first PM internally, never externally.** Against the typical CEO instinct to recruit an experienced PM from outside, Gokul argues the first PM must be converted from inside — because the role is fundamentally about earning trust and legitimizing a new function, not bringing in best practices.

> "I always tell every CEO I meet that is trying to hire their first PM or doing their first product review, you need to ask why. The only question you need to ask is why. Why are you launching this feature?" — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "the best PM candidates rejected the premise completely and they did it in a beautiful way. They went and talked to 10 customers on the street." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

**5. Span of control under 10 shouldn't be allowed.** Against typical management hierarchies, Gokul argues that managing fewer than 10–15 people is not a real job in the AI era. Either be an IC building things, or manage 50+ humans or AI agents — middle management roles are getting compressed out.

> "management has to be a full-time job. What I mean by that is if you manage three, five, 10 people that's not enough. You either need to be managing 50 humans or you need to be an IC." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "span of control less than 10 should not be allowed at any company at this point... And on the company side, don't hire managers as long as possible. Hire doers. Hire builders." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

### Does Not Believe

**1. Margins matter in years 1-2.** Gokul rejects the reflex of dismissing companies because of bad early margins. DoorDash, Spotify, Deliveroo all had bad early margins. What matters is durability and the future ability to raise prices, not current margin structure.

> "you and I both we don't look at margins in year one and two. I mean it doesn't make sense even years four and five." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "I just see so many investors stay being relatively inelastic in terms of their mindset on margin where it's like oh the margins are [bad]... I don't think Door Dash had great gross margins for the first few years either... Spotify didn't have great margins for a very long time" — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "margin is much less, it's not something I worry about. I think durability and defensibility is much more of a worry." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

**2. Proprietary founder access is real.** Gokul explicitly rejects the standard VC pitch of 'proprietary access.' What's proprietary is the value you actually add — counsel is free, the differentiator must be distribution, customer access, or hiring help.

> "There's no proprietary founder access. What is proprietary is your ability to add value. And I think founders, you basically have to I think build if you're just capital and assuming founders will come to you, you're not going to win." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "Most VC pitches look the same. What you want to do is dig one level deeper and talk to the founders themselves and understand for each of the last five companies that they invested in, why did this founder pick uh this firm?" — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

**3. Pure remote works for early-stage startups.** Gokul publicly changed his mind: he used to believe pure remote could work with the right culture, but he's seen companies die because remote founders couldn't iterate fast enough or align on strategy. In-person at least 3 days/week is now his bar.

> "I used to think pure remote would u would scale for early stage companies if you had the right culture, but I don't think that anymore. I think you've got to be in person at least a few days a week." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "I think just seeing a few companies where literally the companies died because the founders were unable to agree... iteration speed just suffers massively if you're pure remote." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

**4. Asking users for solutions.** Gokul rejects the standard 'talk to users' framing when it means asking users what to build. Users will ask for faster horses. The right move is to interview them for stories about their lived journey to uncover problems — solutions come from your own intuition.

> "I have never believed that talking to users is that interesting. I would much rather tell like get people to react to ideas and solutions because... it's not the user's job to tell me like what great ideas are that's my job" — *Gokul Rajaram on Starting a Company in the Age of AI*

> "you interview people and there's a way of interviewing them to uncover problems you never interview them to uncover solutions As you know if you ask people for solution they will ask you they will keep asking faster horses" — *Gokul Rajaram on Starting a Company in the Age of AI*

**5. Staying 12-18 months at a job is fine.** Gokul rejects the modern norm of fast job-hopping. He views serial 12-18 month tenures as one of the biggest red flags in hiring — you cannot have meaningful impact in that window, and people who optimize for the next role rarely build anything.

> "I've been seeing this phenomenon of job hoppers or job optimizers I call them who stay at a job for 12 to 18 months and then they move to the next job... I think that that is one of the biggest red flags as a hiring manager that I see because I don't think you can achieve anything of value." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "Stay at every job long enough to have impact... I think it takes minimum 3 to four years to have impact on a company." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

### Would Not Say

**1. "Let's get consensus on this decision".** Gokul actively rejects consensus as a decision-making mode for important calls. He'd reframe to: who is the one responsible person who owns this?

> "consensus doesn't work. Consensus doesn't work as a decision making tool, especially for very important decisions, and actually for most decisions, because there's no owner" — *Prolific Angel Investor Gokul Rajaram — SPADE*

> "it is that consensus doesn't work don't use consensus agreement is not ownership doesn't work" — *Square Defangs Difficult Decisions*

**2. "The margins are bad, pass".** Gokul wouldn't reject a company on early margins. He'd ask about durability, switching costs, and future pricing power — margins fix themselves if defensibility is real.

> "I just see so many investors stay being relatively inelastic in terms of their mindset on margin where it's like oh the margins are [bad] and it's like I don't... I don't think Door Dash had great gross margins for the first few years either" — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "margin is much less, it's not something I worry about. I think durability and defensibility is much more of a worry." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

**3. "We have proprietary founder access".** Gokul has explicitly called out this as a meaningless VC pitch. He'd describe specifically what value he adds — distribution, hiring, customer intros — rather than claim privileged access.

> "There's no proprietary founder access. What is proprietary is your ability to add value." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "if you're just capital and assuming founders will come to you, you're not going to win. But what you have to offer them is something. What you offer them is something very different and unique." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

**4. "Let's ship this feature and see what happens".** Gokul wouldn't ship without an explicit hypothesis about customer behavior change. Every feature needs a 'why' grounded in a specific behavior the customer will adopt.

> "you should not let any feature go out if there's not a clear hypothesis behind this feature. And the hypothesis has to be articulated in the form of a customer behavior change." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "The only question you need to ask is why. Why are you launching this feature?" — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

**5. "Let's add more gates to onboarding to reduce risk".** Gokul would push back on upfront gating. Risk belongs at the transaction layer, not the signup layer — every gate kills self-serve and loses the rarest thing: someone showing up wanting to use your product.

> "Someone is coming to you and expressing an interest and you're saying you're going to put 10 different barriers. That's the opposite of self-s serve." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "pure self-serve product would never have any reviews of any kind. You're going to be immediately activated. go on and we'll do checks in real time based on what you're doing versus banning you or stopping you." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

### Thinking Patterns

**Reduces complex domains to numbered taxonomies.** Gokul habitually compresses messy strategic questions into clean enumerated frameworks — the 8 moats, 3 ways to make money in ads, 5 dimensions of durability, 3 founder archetypes. The taxonomy becomes a scoring rubric for evaluating companies.

> "I call it the eight modes... data, workflow, regulatory, distribution, ecosystem, network, physical and and scale... you want to take a company and score it across them. Maybe you assign one point to each mode they have." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "There are three fundamental ways to succeed in the ads business. Three and only three. One, you need to own a very coveted uh group of users... Second, you have to drive outcomes... The third way to do it is if you are the exclusive provider" — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

**Reaches for canonical company examples to anchor abstractions.** Almost every principle Gokul states is immediately grounded with 2-4 specific company examples (Service Titan, Faire, Figma, Square Capital, Toast, Palantir). He thinks in case studies, not theory.

> "Service Titan, for example, is a canonical example. It went public last year, great outcome, is still a sub10 billion company or something like that. And they own like 30 they have, if you look at their S1, they have 32 different products." — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "Palunteer does that very well. Palunteer goes to customers and say what's your most important business problem? Oh, here it is. Okay, great. Give us 6 months to solve it. Engage with us. If we can't solve it, fire us." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

**Sources tactics directly from named operator-mentors.** Gokul attributes specific operating moves to specific people he's worked with — Sergey on AdSense gating, Sheryl on contested decisions, Jack on point-of-sale design, Zuck on custom audiences, Eric on decision ownership. He learns by absorbing concrete moves from named operators.

> "Sergey was our sponsor he came and sat in the meetings he said what are you guys building here we're like oh you know website publishers are going to apply... He's like, why do you need to approve them?" — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "we decided to go to Sheryl Sandberg and ask her to be the responsible person to make that decision... Sheryl agreed to become the responsible person for the decision she got all of us in her office around the table" — *Square Defangs Difficult Decisions*

**Pairs north-star metric with check metric to prevent abuse.** Whenever Gokul names a single optimization target, he immediately introduces a guard-rail metric. Incentives drive behavior, and a lone metric will get gamed — so GMV pairs with margin, engagement pairs with ad load, etc.

> "if you tell a team go and optimize this Nstra metric they will do what it is going to go up 100%. But then many things that you don't want to go down could go down... So for example in in the Door Dash case... I could make it grow up by offering by setting delivery fee to zero" — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

> "you basically want a check metric that is maybe a check metric around the health of the customer and a check metric around the health of the company that basically hold this that are the guard rails around this Nstra metric." — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

**Asks 'why this and not three alternatives I'll throw at you'.** When evaluating founders, Gokul probes the idea maze by deliberately introducing alternative approaches the founder didn't pick, to test whether they've studied the history and have a real reason for their wedge.

> "I ask all kinds of random questions to understand why did you approach this because they've taken most founders have a specific approach solving the problem I ask them why couldn't you take this other approach random approach" — *Gokul Rajaram on Starting a Company in the Age of AI*

> "the other one is idea maze. Tell me about how you navigated the idea maze... I will basically throw try to throw them off course or offkilter by asking them five six other ways to solve the same problem" — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*

**Distinguishes structurally different cases inside a category.** Where most investors give one answer, Gokul splits the category — 'two kinds of products: access products and work products'; 'two kinds of software companies: utility-priced vs. data-half-life-long.' The split sharpens the analysis and yields different recommendations per branch.

> "you have two kinds of products. You have access products and you have work products. Access products um is basically seat based uh like I think Chad GB enterprise is a good example and then work products like Harvey are probably more outcome based" — *20VC with Harry Stebbings*

> "There are two or three kinds of software companies. I think the the software companies that are should be the most worried right now is where they are pricing the product based on utility... The companies that are less exposed are ones where the utility is not based on seats but " — *Invest Like the Best EP.456*
